text: Training with reduced carbohydrate availability (train-low) augments cell signalling responses compared to high carbohydrate availability in approximately 73% of studies examined.
Train-low approaches enhance gene expression related to endurance adaptations in approximately 75% of studies examined.
Train-low approaches increase oxidative enzyme activity and protein content in approximately 78% of studies examined.
Despite enhanced molecular and metabolic adaptations, train-low approaches only improve exercise performance in about 37% of studies, with 63% showing no change.
Train-low sessions should be periodized to comprise approximately 30-50% of total training sessions, not applied universally to all sessions.

There exists a glycogen threshold — a specific range of muscle glycogen concentrations that simultaneously permits completion of training workloads and activates molecular signalling for training adaptations.

Carbohydrate availability should be adjusted according to the demands of upcoming training sessions ('fuel for the work required'), with high carbohydrate for high-intensity sessions and low carbohydrate for selected lower-intensity sessions.
When implementing train-low sessions, absolute training intensity must not be compromised — reduced carbohydrate availability should only be applied to sessions where intensity can be maintained.
